original_abstract: 10 A many times heard mantra of solvent degradation management in amine-based post combustion capture is “keep the solvent 11 clean” to minimize solvent consumption. It is assumed that the amine losses would decrease by the removal of metals, 12 degradation products, and reactive trace components which are captured from the flue gas, like NO2 (as potentially driving 13 components of the amine degradation besides dissolved O2). However, this theoretical hypothesis – based on results from 14 laboratory experiments typically generated with fresh amines – disregards the complexity of the solvent matrix, interaction of 15 potential metal catalysts with degradation products and oxidizing agents, and specific chemical requirements which must be 16 fulfilled before a degradation mechanism can proceed. Degradation of the solvent CESAR1 (aqueous solution of 3.0 molar 2- 17 * Corresponding author. Email address: peter.moser@rwe.com © 2023 published by Elsevier. humanized_abstract: 10 A common saying in solvent degradation management of amine-based post combustion capture is “keep the solvent 11 clean” to save the solvent. Removal of metals, degradation products and trace reactants, such as NO 2, which are all contained in the flue gas that is passed through the absorber and are assumed to contribute to amine loss. However, in practice these lab experiments have been performed with fresh amines and do not take into account the actual structure of the solvent matrix, the interactions between potential catalysts and contaminants like degradation products and oxidizing species like NO 2, and the activation energy needed for a degradation pathway to start. This work therefore 16 investigated the CESAR1 (aqueous solution of a 3.0 molar monoethanol amine (MEA) concentration) solvent degradation 17 behavior, focusing on the evolution of carbamates as well as nitriles in the presence of several dissolved iron species and atmospheric 18 oxygen. These parameters were selected as iron and oxygen are present in higher concentrations in the overall process loop.

document.output: 10 A common saying in solvent degradation management of amine-based post combustion capture is “keep the solvent 11 clean” to save the solvent. However, in practice these lab experiments have been performed with fresh amines and do not take into account the actual structure of the solvent matrix, the interactions between potential catalysts and contaminants like degradation products and oxidizing species like NO 2, and the activation energy needed for a degradation pathway to start. This work therefore 16 investigated the CESAR1 (aqueous solution of a 3.0 molar monoethanol amine (MEA) concentration) solvent degradation 17 behavior, focusing on the evolution of carbamates as well as nitriles in the presence of several dissolved iron species and atmospheric 18 oxygen. These parameters were selected as iron and oxygen are present in higher concentrations in the overall process loop.
